label: Acute diarrhea, stable, without blood or dehydration
found: Your clinician diagnosed acute diarrhea that was safe for home care today. We did not find blood in the stool, severe dehydration, severe abdominal pain, or another emergency problem.
home: Drink fluids as your clinician said is safe for you.
Start with foods you can tolerate and advance slowly.
Wash your hands well to avoid spreading infection.
meds: Use diarrhea medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use anti-diarrhea medicine if you develop fever, bloody stool, or severe belly pain unless your clinician told you to.
return: Blood or black stool, severe belly pain, fainting, confusion, or fever.
Signs of dehydration, such as very little urine, dizziness, dry mouth, or inability to keep fluids down.
Symptoms persist longer than expected or worsen despite the plan.
follow: Follow up with primary care or urgent care if symptoms are not improving, recur, or you were told to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of acute uncomplicated diarrhea.
Stable for discharge and able to hydrate, with no blood in stool, severe abdominal pain, dehydration, C diff risk, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or high-risk host pathway.
exclusion: Blood or black stool, severe abdominal pain, dehydration, inability to tolerate oral fluids, fever with severe illness, sepsis, or unstable vitals.
Recent antibiotics/C diff concern, immunocompromised host, elderly frail pathway, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, travel/outbreak pathway needing public-health handling, or specialist-directed plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports fluid replacement, gradual diet advancement, and escalation for dehydration, blood in stool, fever, severe abdominal pain, or prolonged symptoms. Autonomous Phase 1801-1850 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated acute diarrhea without bleeding, dehydration, severe abdominal pain, C diff risk, sepsis/unstable vitals, high-risk host, pregnancy/pediatric, travel/outbreak, or specialist-directed pathway.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated acute diarrhea
stable and able to hydrate with no blood/black stool or dehydration documented
no severe abdominal pain, C diff risk, high-risk host, pregnancy, pediatric, travel/outbreak, or specialist-directed pathway

label: Uncomplicated laryngitis without airway or deep-neck red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit laryngitis, irritation or swelling around the voice box.
found: Your clinician diagnosed uncomplicated laryngitis. We did not find breathing trouble, drooling, deep neck infection concern, or another emergency problem today.
home: Avoid smoke, vaping, alcohol fumes, and other throat irritants.
meds: Use throat or pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Antibiotics are not part of this instruction unless your clinician gave you a separate plan.
return: Trouble breathing, noisy breathing, drooling, inability to swallow, or neck swelling.
Severe throat pain, stiff neck, fever with worsening illness, or coughing blood.
Hoarseness lasts longer than expected or keeps returning.
follow: Follow up with primary care or ENT if symptoms persist, recur, or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated laryngitis.
No airway symptoms, drooling, deep neck infection concern, severe throat pain, hemoptysis, neck mass, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed ENT plan.
exclusion: Airway symptoms, stridor, drooling, inability to swallow, deep neck infection concern, epiglottitis concern, severe throat pain, neck swelling, hemoptysis, or systemic illness.
Immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, voice-professional/specialist-directed plan, or prolonged/recurrent hoarseness requiring ENT evaluation.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports laryngitis framing, hoarseness/voice-loss symptoms, voice rest, fluids, humidified air, and avoidance of irritants. Autonomous Phase 1801-1850 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated laryngitis without airway/deep neck red flags, hemoptysis, systemic illness, high-risk host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, prolonged/recurrent hoarseness pathway, or specialist-directed ENT plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated laryngitis
no airway symptoms, trouble breathing, drooling, neck swelling, or deep neck infection concern documented
no hemoptysis, prolonged/recurrent hoarseness, high-risk host, pregnancy, pediatric, or specialist-directed pathway

label: Uncomplicated plantar fasciitis without trauma or neurovascular red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit plantar fasciitis, irritation of tissue on the bottom of the foot.
found: Your clinician diagnosed uncomplicated plantar fasciitis. We did not find fracture concern, infection, diabetic-foot risk, or neurovascular problem today.
home: Rest from activities that worsen the pain.
Use supportive shoes or inserts if recommended.
Do gentle stretches only as instructed and stop if pain worsens.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Avoid medicines your clinician told you to avoid.
return: Unable to walk, new numbness, weakness, cold/blue foot, or severe worsening pain.
Fever, spreading redness, pus, open wound, or red streaks.
New injury, fracture concern, or symptoms not improving with the plan.
follow: Follow up with primary care, podiatry, sports medicine, or orthopedics if pain persists or walking remains limited.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated plantar fasciitis.
No trauma/fracture concern, infection, open wound, diabetic-foot/PVD/neuropathy risk, neurovascular compromise, inability to walk, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Trauma, fracture/dislocation concern, inability to bear weight, neurovascular compromise, open wound, cellulitis, abscess, fever, or systemic illness.
Diabetic foot risk, neuropathy, PVD, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed podiatry/orthopedic plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports plantar-fasciitis framing, heel/bottom-foot pain, and supportive measures including rest, stretching, footwear changes, and clinician-directed pain control. Autonomous Phase 1801-1850 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated plantar fasciitis without trauma/fracture concern, infection, diabetic-foot/PVD/neuropathy risk, neurovascular compromise, inability to walk, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated plantar fasciitis
no trauma/fracture concern, open wound, diabetic foot, or neurovascular red flags documented
no inability to walk, infection, pregnancy, pediatric, or specialist-directed pathway

label: Minor superficial burn, small area, without high-risk features
summary: Your symptoms fit a minor superficial burn.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a small minor burn. We did not find a deep burn, infection, inhalation injury, high-risk location, or another emergency problem today.
home: Keep the burn clean and protected.
Avoid more heat, sun, friction, or chemical exposure to the area.
Do not pop blisters or put unsafe substances on the burn.
meds: Use burn cream, dressing supplies, or pain medicine only as prescribed or recommended.
Do not apply butter, oils, toothpaste, or harsh chemicals.
return: Increasing redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, fever, or worsening pain.
Trouble breathing, cough after smoke exposure, face burn, hand/genital burn, circumferential burn, or deep/white/charred skin.
Burn is larger than expected, crosses a joint, or is not healing.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, burn care, or the ED as instructed, especially if pain, redness, or healing is not improving.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of small superficial minor burn.

No deep/partial/full-thickness burn concern, circumferential burn, face/hand/genital/major joint location, inhalation injury, infection, chemical/electrical burn, high-risk host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed burn plan.
exclusion: Deep, white, charred, circumferential, large, face/hand/genital/major joint burn, inhalation injury, chemical/electrical burn, infection, fever, or systemic illness.
Diabetes, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, unreliable wound care, or specialist-directed burn plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports minor-burn aftercare concepts, cooling/protection, avoiding blister disruption or unsafe substances, and escalation for large, deep, infected, high-risk-location, chemical/electrical, inhalation-associated, or high-risk-host burns. Autonomous Phase 1801-1850 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed small superficial minor burn without high-risk depth/location/mechanism, inhalation injury, infection, high-risk host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed burn plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of small superficial minor burn
no deep burn, inhalation injury, high-risk location, or burn infection documented
no chemical/electrical burn, high-risk host, pregnancy, pediatric, or specialist-directed burn pathway

label: Uncomplicated muscle strain without fracture or neurovascular red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit a muscle strain.
found: Your clinician diagnosed an uncomplicated muscle strain. We did not find fracture concern, tendon rupture, neurovascular problem, or another emergency problem today.
home: Rest from activities that worsen pain.
Use ice or heat only as instructed.
Return to activity gradually as symptoms improve.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Avoid medicines your clinician told you to avoid.
return: Severe worsening pain, tight swelling, numbness, weakness, color change, or trouble using the limb.
New injury, fracture concern, inability to walk or use the limb, or symptoms not improving.
Fever, redness, or swelling that suggests infection.
follow: Follow up with primary care, sports medicine, occupational health, or orthopedics if function is not improving.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated muscle strain.
No fracture/dislocation concern, tendon rupture, compartment syndrome, neurovascular compromise, high-energy trauma, open wound, infection, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Fracture/dislocation concern, tendon rupture, severe pain, compartment syndrome, neurovascular compromise, inability to use limb, high-energy trauma, open wound, infection, or systemic illness.
Pregnancy/pediatric pathway, anticoagulation/bleeding concern, or specialist-directed orthopedic plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports sprain/strain framing and supportive care concepts. Autonomous Phase 1801-1850 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated muscle strain without fracture/dislocation concern, tendon rupture, compartment syndrome, neurovascular compromise, high-energy trauma, open wound/infection, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated muscle strain
no fracture/dislocation concern, tendon rupture, compartment syndrome, or neurovascular red flags documented
no high-energy trauma, open wound/infection, pregnancy, pediatric, or specialist-directed pathway
